title: An Introduction to Magic Squares
text:
1.Magic Squares
A magic square is a square grid of usually positive natural numbers whose sums of the numbers in each row, column, and diagonals are the same.
Here are a few examples,



the famous one


\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
17 & 113 & 47 \\ \hline
89 & 59 & 29 \\ \hline
71 & 5 & 101
\end{array}
\]
This one is made of primes. It’s sum is 177
\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c}
7 & 12 & 1 & 14 \\ \hline
2 & 13 & 8 & 11 \\ \hline
16 & 3 & 10 & 5 \\ \hline
9 & 6 & 15 & 4
\end{array}
\]
This one is 4 by 4 and sums to 34


2.Magic square of squares
A magic square is a magic square of square if the numbers in each cell can be squared and the resulting grid is also a magic square.
No 3 by 3 magic square of squares has been found, but other magic square of squares have been.
Here’s one found by Euler. Its sum is 8,515. \[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c}
68^{2} & 29^{2} & 41^{2} & 37^{2} \\ \hline
17^{2} & 31^{2} & 79^{2} & 32^{2} \\ \hline
59^{2} & 28^{2} & 23^{2} & 61^{2} \\ \hline
11^{2} & 77^{2} & 8^{2} & 49^{2}
\end{array}
\]
